required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud or Independent] Stories of people who carried responsibility young

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud or Independent] Farmer Boy
suggested.author: Laura Ingalls Wilder

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud or Independent] The Sign of the Beaver
suggested.author: Elizabeth George Speare

suggested.author: Gary Paulsen

supplementary.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud or Independent] Biographies of young people with real responsibility

competence_markers.description: They complete their assigned chores independently, without reminders, on most days — and notice when they have forgotten rather than waiting to be told
They can articulate the difference between wants and needs and make real choices based on the distinction — letting go of wants without prolonged protest
They wait for extended periods (30+ minutes, "tomorrow," "next week") without repeated asking, emotional escalation, or visible misery designed to change the outcome
They respond to correction with the four-step pattern — listen, acknowledge, correct, move on — on most occasions, without habitual argument, meltdown, or sulking
They manage self-directed task blocks productively, choosing a reasonable order and completing all tasks within the given time
They use unstructured time and waiting periods productively without needing adult direction
They bring their own observations to the weekly review — patterns they noticed, mistakes they identified, struggles they name without being asked
They own mistakes proactively — coming forward about errors before being discovered, at least some of the time
They demonstrate growing self-awareness about their own patterns — when they avoid things, what triggers poor responses, what conditions produce their best work

narration_prompts.prompt: What are your chores, and what does it look like when each one is done right?
narration_prompts.detail: How often this week did you do them without being reminded? Be honest.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time this week when you wanted something but recognized it was a want, not a need.
Describe a time you were corrected this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What did the person say? What was your first impulse? What did you actually do? Would you handle it differently if it happened again?

narration_prompts.prompt: When you had free time or had to wait this week, what did you do with it?
narration_prompts.detail: Did you find something useful, or did you need someone to direct you?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a mistake you made this week that you owned before anyone pointed it out.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? How did it feel to come forward?

narration_prompts.prompt: When you got to choose the order of your tasks, what did you choose and why?
narration_prompts.detail: Did it work well? What would you change?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is one pattern you noticed about yourself this week?
narration_prompts.detail: Something you keep doing — good or bad — that you are starting to see clearly.

cross_connections.description: A2's self-directed scheduling is the direct precursor to B1's full daily planning. The student who can manage task order within a block is ready to begin managing an entire day. A2 completion is the immediate prerequisite for B1.
Owning mistakes before being caught is the behavioral foundation of integrity. A2 and IC1.3-A reinforce each other — the honesty practiced here is the same honesty IC1.3 formalizes.
Responding to correction without drama is directly connected to the ability to process failure without collapse. A2's correction-response training builds the same circuitry IC1.4 develops.
A2's self-monitoring — noticing your own patterns — is the behavioral cousin of IC1.6's interior self-examination. Both develop the capacity to observe oneself honestly.
A2's first self-directed scheduling connects to IC2.1's systematic approach to daily structure. As the student begins choosing task order, they encounter the practical architecture of time use.

Responding to correction, managing disappointment, and waiting without meltdown are all emotional regulation skills. A2 builds them through daily practice; IC3.1 develops them with explicit awareness.
The self-monitoring and weekly review practices in A2 begin generating material for written reflection, connecting to the developing literacy track.
The self-regulation and self-direction built in A2 transfer directly to physical training — the discipline to push through discomfort, to follow a training plan, to self-correct form.

content.why_this_matters:
You have already learned to follow a routine. You know what it means to make your bed, finish a task, and wait when you are told to wait. That was A1 — the first packet in this entire framework. It gave you the structure you needed when you were younger.

Now the question changes.

A1 asked: *Can you do what you are supposed to do when someone sets it up for you?* A2 asks: *Can you do what you are supposed to do when no one is standing over you?*

This is a real shift, and it is not small. There is a difference between a child who completes a chore because a parent is watching and a child who completes it because it is theirs and they know it needs to be done. There is a difference between a child who waits because they were told to wait and a child who recognizes, on their own, that this is a moment for patience. There is a difference between a child who stops misbehaving when corrected and a child who hears the correction, understands it, and changes without turning it into a battle.

This packet builds the bridge between having good habits because your environment demands them and having good habits because you are becoming the kind of person who carries them. That bridge is what separates people who behave well under supervision from people who behave well because of who they are.

Everything that comes after this — planning your own time in B1, keeping commitments under pressure in B2, governing yourself without any external enforcement in Standing Ground — depends on what you build here. A2 is where external structure begins to become internal structure. It is where the scaffolding starts to come down, and you find out whether the wall can stand on its own.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Independent chore completion** — owning a task from start to finish without reminders, including knowing when it needs to be done, doing it to standard, and recognizing when it is truly complete
2. **Deepened want-versus-need discrimination** — moving beyond simple categorization to making real choices based on the distinction
3. **Extended waiting capacity** — tolerating longer delays, managing disappointment when plans change, and occupying yourself productively during waiting periods
4. **Responding to correction without drama** — hearing what you did wrong, accepting it, adjusting, and moving on without emotional escalation, argument, or prolonged sulking
5. **First self-directed scheduling** — beginning to decide the order of your own tasks within a given block of time
6. **Self-monitoring** — starting to notice your own patterns: when you avoid things, when you rush, when you do your best work, when you fall apart
7. **Ownership of mistakes without prompting** — beginning to identify and acknowledge your own errors before being told
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Owning the Chore — From Reminder to Responsibility

In A1, you had a chore. Someone probably reminded you about it at least some of the time. That was appropriate — you were learning the habit.

Now the reminder stops.

Your chore — or chores, because by this point you should have more than one — are yours in the fullest sense. You know what needs to be done. You know when it needs to be done. You know what "done" looks like. And you do it, without being asked. Not because someone is watching. Not because there will be a consequence. Because it is yours.

This sounds simple. It is the hardest transition in this entire packet.

The difference between "I do my chore when reminded" and "I do my chore because it is mine" is the difference between compliance and ownership. Compliance is external. Someone else holds the responsibility, and you just do the action. Ownership is internal. You hold the responsibility. If the chore does not get done, you feel it — not because you got in trouble, but because something that was yours was left undone.

**What ownership looks like in practice:**

You feed the animals in the morning. No one says "time to feed the animals." You know it is morning. You know the animals are hungry. You go feed them. If you forget, you notice — not when your parent notices, but when you notice. And you go do it immediately, without needing to be told.

You clear the table after dinner. The meal ends. You do not wait to be asked. You do not negotiate whose turn it is. You know the table is your responsibility. You clear it.

You keep your room in working order. Not spotless — working order. You can find your things. The floor is navigable. The bed is made. This is not a weekly event prompted by an adult's frustration. It is daily maintenance that belongs to you.

**The test of ownership is what happens when no one is watching.** If your chore only gets done when someone is home to see it, you have compliance. If it gets done regardless — on the morning your parent sleeps in, on the afternoon everyone is busy elsewhere — you have ownership. That is what A2 builds.

### Part 2: Want Versus Need — Making Real Choices

In A1, you learned to sort your desires into two categories: things you want and things you need. That was a cognitive exercise — you were learning to tell the difference.

Now you begin to act on the difference.

The child who knows the difference between a want and a need but demands both equally has not yet learned what the distinction is for. The distinction exists so you can make decisions. When you cannot have everything — and you cannot, not ever, not at any age — you choose needs first. When resources are limited — time, money, energy, your parents' patience — needs come before wants. And wants that conflict with needs get set aside.

**This plays out in daily life:**

You want to keep playing. You need to finish your work. The work comes first. Not because someone forces you, but because you understand the priority.

You want a particular food for dinner. What is available is something else. You eat what is available without complaint, because hunger is the need and preference is the want.

You want to stay up later. You need sleep because tomorrow has demands. You go to bed.

You want something at the store. It is not in the budget or the plan. You leave without it. Not with a tantrum. Not with prolonged negotiation. You recognize it as a want, and you let it go.

**The deeper lesson:** Letting go of a want is not losing. It is governing yourself. Every time you recognize a want, name it honestly, and choose not to pursue it because the moment does not call for it, you are doing something most adults never master. You are subordinating impulse to judgment. This is not deprivation — it is power. The person who can walk away from what they want is more free than the person who cannot.

This does not mean wants are bad. It does not mean you never get what you want. It means you develop the capacity to want something and not be controlled by the wanting. That capacity will serve you in every domain of your life, for the rest of your life.

### Part 3: Learning to Wait — Longer, Harder, and Without Complaint

In A1, you practiced waiting for short intervals. "Dinner is in twenty minutes." "We will go after lunch." Those were the beginning.

A2 extends the challenge. The waits get longer. The disappointments get more real. And the expectation is not just that you wait, but that you wait well — without repeated asking, without visible misery designed to pressure someone into giving in, and without treating the waiting period as dead time.

**What waiting well looks like:**

You are told that the trip to your friend's house is tomorrow, not today. You do not ask again. You do not mope for the rest of the afternoon. You find something useful or enjoyable to do with the time you have.

Something you were looking forward to gets cancelled. You are disappointed — that is honest and appropriate. But the disappointment does not become a performance. You feel it, you express it briefly if you need to, and you move on to what is next.

You want your mentor's attention, but they are in the middle of something. You wait. You do not interrupt. You do not hover. You find something to do until they are available.

A long-term goal requires patience — saving for something, working toward a skill milestone, waiting for a season to change. You sustain effort across the waiting period without losing motivation just because the result is not immediate.

**The hardest part of waiting is not the delay itself. It is what you do with yourself during the delay.** The person who can only be content when they are getting what they want is a prisoner of their circumstances. The person who can be productive and at peace during a wait — who can occupy themselves, contribute, or simply be still — is free regardless of what is happening around them.

**Productive waiting** is a skill. When you have to wait, ask yourself: what can I do right now that is useful? What can I work on? What can I read, build, practice, or clean? The wait becomes a gift of time instead of a punishment of delay. This reframe is not natural — it is trained. You train it by doing it, over and over, until it becomes your default response to "not yet."

### Part 4: Responding to Correction Without Drama

Everyone makes mistakes. Everyone does things wrong. Everyone gets corrected. The question is not whether you will be corrected — you will, often, for years to come — but how you respond when it happens.

There are three common wrong responses to correction:

**The Argument.** You are told you did something wrong. Instead of hearing it, you argue. "But I didn't!" "That's not fair!" "You never said that!" The argument is a defense mechanism — it protects you from the discomfort of being wrong by turning the correction into a debate. But the debate does not change the fact. You still did the thing. And now, in addition to the original mistake, you have added a second problem: you are the kind of person who cannot hear correction without fighting.

**The Meltdown.** You are told you did something wrong. Instead of hearing it, you cry, shut down, or fall apart. The meltdown turns the correction into a crisis — and now the adult has to manage your emotions instead of addressing the behavior. Some children learn, without intending to, that a meltdown is an effective way to end a correction. It is. But the behavior does not change, and the pattern — mistake, meltdown, comfort, repeat — becomes a trap.

**The Sulk.** You are told you did something wrong. You do not argue. You do not cry. But you withdraw. You go silent. You radiate resentment. The sulk is a slow-burn protest — a way of punishing the person who corrected you by making the atmosphere uncomfortable. It is harder to identify than the argument or the meltdown, but it is just as much a failure to receive correction.

**The right response has four steps:**

1. **Listen.** Hear what is being said. Do not prepare your defense while the other person is talking.
2. **Acknowledge.** "You're right" or "I understand" or simply a nod. The point is that the correction has landed — it has been received, not deflected.
3. **Correct.** Do the thing differently. Fix the mistake. Change the behavior. This is the part that actually matters.
4. **Move on.** It is done. The correction happened. You adjusted. There is no need to carry it forward as a grievance, a wound, or a reason to be miserable for the rest of the day.

This sequence — listen, acknowledge, correct, move on — is one of the most valuable behavioral patterns a person can develop. Adults who can do this are trusted with more responsibility, given harder assignments, and promoted into leadership. Adults who cannot do this — who argue, melt down, or sulk every time they receive feedback — remain limited, regardless of their other abilities.

You are seven, eight, or nine years old. You are not expected to receive every correction with the calm of a seasoned leader. But you are expected to practice. Every correction is a repetition. Every time you hear feedback without drama, the pattern gets stronger. Over years, it becomes who you are — a person who can be told they are wrong and respond with adjustment instead of defense.

### Part 5: First Self-Directed Scheduling

Until now, your schedule has been given to you. Your morning routine was established. Your schoolwork time was set. Your chore times were determined by the household rhythm.

A2 introduces a small but significant change: within certain blocks of time, you begin to decide the order of your own tasks.

This is not the full daily planning that B1 will teach you. It is the first step — a limited version with clear boundaries.

**What this looks like:**

Your mentor tells you: "This morning you need to do your math, your reading, and your handwriting practice. You have until lunch. You decide what order to do them in."

That is it. Three tasks. A fixed deadline. Your choice is the sequence.

This sounds trivial. It is not. The act of looking at three tasks and deciding which to do first requires you to think about yourself — your energy, your preferences, your tendency to avoid certain things. It requires you to make a plan and then follow it. And it introduces the earliest form of a skill that will become central to your life: managing your own time.

**Common patterns you will discover about yourself:**

Some students always do the easiest thing first. This feels good in the moment but means the hardest task is always waiting at the end, when energy is lowest. Some students do the hardest thing first and find the rest of the morning easier. Some students alternate. There is no single right answer, but there is a right process: notice what you do, notice what works, and adjust.

**The boundaries matter.** Self-directed scheduling at this stage does not mean you decide whether to do the work. The work is not optional. What is optional is the order. The freedom is real but bounded — and that is exactly right for where you are. Full autonomy comes later, after you have demonstrated that you can manage limited autonomy well.

Your mentor may also begin giving you short blocks of unstructured time — thirty minutes or an hour — where the only instruction is: "Use this time well." No specific task. No entertainment provided. You decide what to do. This is the earliest practice of self-direction, and it reveals a great deal about the habits you are building. The student who immediately finds something productive or meaningful to do is developing self-governance. The student who wanders, complains of boredom, or waits to be told what to do has more work to do on this front.

### Part 6: Noticing Yourself — The Beginning of Self-Monitoring

Self-regulation requires self-awareness. You cannot change a pattern you do not see.

A2 begins a practice that will deepen through every tier of this framework: the practice of noticing yourself. Not judging — noticing. Observing your own behavior the way you would observe someone else's, with honesty and without drama.

**What this looks like:**

You notice that you always avoid your math work. Not because someone points it out — because you see it yourself. "I keep doing everything else first, and then I rush through math at the end."

You notice that you get angry when your sibling touches your things. Not after the blowup — during it, or at least immediately after. "I just yelled, and the real reason was not what I said it was."

You notice that you do your best work in the morning and your worst work after lunch. Not because a study told you — because you paid attention to your own experience.

You notice that when you are corrected, your first impulse is to argue. You see the impulse before you act on it. Maybe you still argue sometimes. But you saw it — and seeing it is the first step toward choosing differently.

**The weekly review from A1 continues, but it changes.** In A1, the weekly review was mostly the mentor asking questions and the child answering. In A2, the student begins to bring their own observations. "This week I noticed that I kept forgetting to feed the dog on Wednesday mornings. I think it's because Wednesday is different and it breaks my routine." That kind of self-observation — unprompted, specific, honest — is the sign that self-monitoring is developing.

This is not therapy. It is not navel-gazing. It is the practical skill of paying attention to your own patterns so you can govern them. The person who does not notice their patterns is governed by them. The person who notices has a choice. A2 is where that noticing begins.

### Part 7: Owning Mistakes Before You Are Caught

In A1, the emphasis was on truth-telling when asked. A2 raises the standard: you begin to own your mistakes before someone else identifies them.

The difference matters. A child who admits to breaking a dish when asked, "Did you break this dish?" is being honest. That is good. But a child who comes to a parent and says, "I broke a dish. I'm sorry. I'll clean it up," — without being asked, without being caught — is doing something harder and more important. They are taking ownership.

This does not come naturally. The natural impulse when you make a mistake is to hope no one notices. The natural impulse is to stay quiet, to let it pass, to fix it quietly and move on as if it did not happen. Sometimes that impulse wins. What A2 builds is the competing impulse — the one that says, "This is mine. I did this. I need to say so."

**Why this matters beyond the obvious:** Trust is built in these moments. When the people around you know that you will tell them when something goes wrong — without waiting to be discovered — they trust you more. They give you more responsibility. They worry less. And that trust becomes the foundation for the increasing independence that B1 and B2 and Standing Ground will require.

The practice is straightforward: when you make a mistake, say so. Do not wait. Do not hide it. Do not minimize it. State what happened, take responsibility, and move to repair. Your mentor's job is to receive this honestly — without punishment that discourages future disclosure and without excessive praise that makes honesty feel performative. The goal is that owning mistakes becomes unremarkable. It is simply what you do.
content.formative_practices:
These are ongoing practices — daily, weekly, and as situations arise. They are not exercises to complete once. They are the repeated actions through which A2 formation happens.

### Practice 1: Independent Chore Completion [Daily]
You have at least two chores that are fully yours. No one reminds you. No one checks behind you unless there is a reason to suspect the standard has slipped. You know what needs to be done, when, and to what standard. Track yourself: how many days this week did you complete your chores without any reminder? Be honest. The number is information, not a grade.

### Practice 2: The Correction Response [As Situations Arise]
When you are corrected, practice the four-step response: listen, acknowledge, correct, move on. After each correction, your mentor may ask: "How did you handle that?" Not as a test — as a reflection. Over time, you begin to ask yourself that question before your mentor does.

### Practice 3: Want-Need Sorting in Real Time [Daily]
When you feel a strong desire — for a treat, a purchase, more screen time, an activity — pause and categorize it before you ask for it. "This is a want." You can still ask. But the sorting happens first, inside you, before it becomes a request. The goal is that the sorting becomes automatic.

### Practice 4: Self-Directed Task Blocks [Daily or Near-Daily]
At least once a day, you are given a set of tasks and a time block, and you decide the order. After the block, review: Did you finish everything? Did your chosen order work well? What would you change? This is the earliest practice of time ownership.

### Practice 5: Productive Waiting [As Opportunities Arise]
When you are told to wait — for a meal, for an activity, for a person — find something to do without being told. Not a screen. Something real: read, draw, build, practice a skill, help with something nearby. The measure is: can you turn a waiting period into a useful period without adult direction?

### Practice 6: Weekly Self-Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
The weekly review continues from A1, but now you are expected to bring your own observations — not just answer questions. Before the review, think about your week: Where did you own your responsibilities well? Where did you slip? What patterns did you notice? What was hardest? Come to the review with at least one observation that your mentor did not have to prompt. Keep the review to 10-15 minutes.

### Practice 7: Mistake Ownership [As Situations Arise]
When you make a mistake — break something, forget something, do something wrong — practice coming forward before being asked. State what happened. Take responsibility. Move to repair. Track this informally: did you own it, or did someone else have to find it?

### Practice 8: Read-Aloud or Independent Reading of Character Stories [Weekly]
Continue the weekly practice from A1. At this stage, the stories can be longer and more complex. The discussions go deeper: What did the person sacrifice? What would have happened if they had chosen differently? Have you ever faced a smaller version of the same choice?

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### The Gradual Release of Responsibility

A2 is structured around a pedagogical principle that applies far beyond this packet: gradual release. The adult does it, then the adult does it with the child, then the child does it with the adult watching, then the child does it alone. A1 was stages one through three. A2 is stage four.

The transition must be deliberate, not accidental. You are not stepping back because you are busy or tired. You are stepping back because the child is ready for the next degree of independence, and that degree will not develop unless you create space for it.

**Practical guidance:**

- **Stop reminding.** Pick one chore the child has been doing reliably with occasional reminders. Stop the reminders entirely. Say nothing. Observe what happens. If the chore gets done, the child has internalized it. If it does not, wait until a natural consequence reveals the gap, then address it once: "The dog wasn't fed this morning. That's your responsibility. What happened?" Do not lecture. Do not add reminders back. Let the child solve it.
- **Extend the delay before intervening.** When you see the child making a mistake — choosing the wrong task order, starting a chore poorly, forgetting a step — wait longer than feels comfortable before stepping in. Often the child will self-correct if given time. If they do not, the consequence of the mistake becomes the teacher. Your intervention should be the last resort, not the first response.
- **Create genuine choice within bounded structures.** "You have three tasks and two hours" is a bounded choice. The child experiences real decision-making without the risk of choosing "do nothing." Gradually expand the boundaries: more tasks, longer blocks, fewer constraints. The expansion follows demonstrated competence, not age alone.
- **Tolerate regression.** The child who independently completed chores last week may need a reminder this week. This is normal. Development is not linear. The question is whether the overall trajectory across months is toward greater independence. A bad week is not a failure. A bad month might be a signal that the step-back was premature.

### Emotional Consistency During Correction

A2 places heavy emphasis on the child's response to correction. But the child's response is shaped, overwhelmingly, by the adult's delivery.

If your corrections are calm, specific, and brief, the child has the best possible conditions for developing a healthy correction response. If your corrections are emotionally loaded — frustrated, sarcastic, disappointed-in-a-way-that-becomes-its-own-punishment — the child is responding to your emotional state, not to the content of the correction. They cannot practice "listen, acknowledge, correct, move on" if the correction itself is a prolonged emotional event.

**The correction standard for A2:**

1. State the behavior, not the character: "You left the table without clearing your plate" — not "You're being lazy."
2. State the expectation: "Your responsibility is to clear your place after every meal."
3. Direct the correction: "Go clear your plate now."
4. Stop talking. Do not explain why the rule exists (they know). Do not add a lecture about how many times this has happened. Do not express disappointment. The correction is complete. Follow through to verify it is done, and move on.

If the child argues, do not engage the argument. Repeat the direction once: "Go clear your plate." If the child melts down, wait for the meltdown to pass, then direct the correction again. If the child sulks, ignore the sulk — do not reward it with attention or comfort — and verify the correction is completed.

The goal is not a child who never resists correction. The goal is a child whose resistance decreases in frequency, duration, and intensity over time, because the correction environment is predictable, proportionate, and non-dramatic.

### Modeling Self-Monitoring

A2 asks the child to begin noticing their own patterns. This is an unusual skill to develop at this age, and it develops best when the child sees it modeled.

Share your own self-observations in natural, brief ways: "I noticed I keep putting off that phone call. I think I'm avoiding it because it's going to be a hard conversation. I'm going to do it now." This is not a performance. It is not a teaching moment you construct artificially. It is you, living with the same self-awareness you are asking the child to develop, and letting them see it.

When the child brings a self-observation — "I noticed I always leave my math until last" — receive it with interest, not instruction. Do not immediately tell them what to do about it. Ask: "Why do you think that is? How does it work out when you do that?" Let them analyze their own pattern before you offer guidance. The observation itself is the achievement. The solution can follow later.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Independent Chore Completion

**Developing well:** The child completes assigned chores without reminders on most days. When they forget, they notice the gap themselves — often later in the day — and correct it without being told. The standard of completion is consistent: the chore is done fully, not partially. The child may occasionally need a check-in ("How are the animals this morning?") but does not need explicit direction.

**Developing slowly:** The child still requires reminders for most chore completions. When the reminder is removed, the chore does not happen. The child does not notice the gap until it is pointed out. Chores are completed partially — the minimum that avoids immediate consequence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the reminder actually been removed, or are you still providing subtle prompts (glancing at the chore chart, asking "Have you done everything you need to do?")? Is the chore well-defined — does the child know exactly what "done" looks like? Is there a natural consequence that makes the gap visible (hungry animals, dirty dishes piling up), or does the gap only become visible when you point it out? Have you given this enough time? Independence develops over weeks, not days.

### 2. Want vs. Need — Applied Decision-Making

**Developing well:** The child categorizes desires accurately without prompting and makes choices accordingly. When told "not today" about a want, they accept it without prolonged negotiation. They can articulate why they are choosing to let go of a want in favor of a need or a priority. The sorting is becoming automatic — you hear the child say things like "I want it, but I don't need it" without being asked.

**Developing slowly:** The child can categorize when asked but does not self-categorize. Every want is treated with equal urgency. "Not today" produces extended negotiation, emotional escalation, or sulking. The distinction between want and need has not translated into actual behavior change.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you consistently maintaining the boundary once you say no? If "not today" sometimes becomes "fine" under pressure, the child is learning that persistence converts wants into gets. Are you modeling the distinction yourself — do you talk about your own wants and needs and the choices you make? Are you allowing some wants to be fulfilled? The distinction should not become a mechanism where only needs are ever met — the child needs to see that wants are legitimate, just not always prioritized.

### 3. Extended Waiting Capacity

**Developing well:** The child can wait for meaningful periods — thirty minutes, a few hours, "tomorrow," "next week" — without repeated asking or emotional deterioration. They occupy themselves productively during the wait. When plans change or anticipated events are postponed, they are disappointed but recover quickly and move to the next thing.

**Developing slowly:** The child asks repeatedly during any wait. Emotional escalation accompanies delays longer than a few minutes. Changed plans produce meltdowns disproportionate to the change. Waiting periods are spent in visible misery or in repeated attempts to renegotiate the timeline.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are wait times age-appropriate? A seven-year-old who can wait until tomorrow is doing well. A nine-year-old who cannot wait thirty minutes has a gap. Are you being specific about the wait? "Later" is harder than "after lunch" — concrete endpoints are more manageable than vague ones. Does the child have access to activities during the wait, or are they in a context where productive waiting is genuinely difficult? Is the waiting practice happening in a variety of contexts (home, errands, outings), or only in one?

### 4. Correction Response

**Developing well:** The child hears correction and responds without habitual argument, meltdown, or extended sulking. They may occasionally resist — that is normal — but the pattern is toward hearing, acknowledging, correcting, and moving on. Recovery time after correction is getting shorter: what once produced a thirty-minute sulk now produces a brief frown and compliance. The child sometimes acknowledges the correction verbally: "Okay, I'll fix it."

**Developing slowly:** Every correction produces a predictable negative response — argument, tears, withdrawal, or some combination. The pattern is not improving over weeks. Recovery time is long: a correction at 10 AM affects the child's mood and behavior for hours. The child is more focused on the unfairness of being corrected than on the behavior that prompted it.

**Critical diagnostic:** Before diagnosing the child, audit your own correction pattern. Are your corrections calm, specific, and brief? Or are they emotionally loaded, lengthy, or variable in intensity? If the child's correction-response is not improving, the first place to look is the correction itself. A child who receives consistent, proportionate, non-dramatic correction will develop a healthy response over time. A child who receives unpredictable, emotionally charged correction is responding to the unpredictability, not the content.

**Specific red flags that suggest the issue is the correction environment, not the child:**
- The child responds well to correction from one adult and poorly from another — the difference is in the adult, not the child
- The child's resistance is worse on days when the adult is stressed or tired — they are reading and responding to your state
- The child handles correction well for minor things but falls apart for major things — this may indicate that "major" corrections come with disproportionate emotional weight

### 5. Self-Directed Task Blocks

**Developing well:** The child can look at a set of tasks, choose an order, and complete them within the allotted time. They are beginning to make strategic choices — doing the hardest thing first when they have energy, or grouping similar tasks together. When a chosen order does not work well, they recognize it in the post-block review: "I should have done math first because I was more focused then."

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot choose an order without adult input. They stall at the beginning of the block, unable to decide. Or they begin with the easiest/most enjoyable task every time and run out of time before completing harder tasks. The post-block review produces no self-awareness: "It was fine" or "I don't know" when asked what they would change.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the number of tasks appropriate? Start with two or three, not five. Is the time block realistic for the tasks involved? Is the child aware of the deadline (a visible clock or timer)? Have you modeled task-ordering out loud — "I need to make that call, write that email, and prepare dinner. I'm going to make the call first because I've been putting it off"?

### 6. Productive Use of Unstructured Time

**Developing well:** When given free time or told to wait, the child finds something to do without adult direction — reads, draws, builds, practices, helps with something nearby. They do not immediately seek a screen or complain of boredom. The activity may be simple, but it is self-initiated.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot occupy themselves without external direction. Free time produces immediate complaints of boredom, requests for screens, or aimless wandering that disrupts others. The child waits for someone to tell them what to do.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the environment set up for success? A child surrounded by accessible activities (books, building materials, art supplies, outdoor space) has an easier time than a child in a barren space. Has screen access been appropriately limited? A child who knows a screen is available and being withheld will lobby for it rather than finding alternatives. Is the child getting enough unstructured time to develop this capacity, or is every moment of their day scheduled by adults?

### 7. Self-Observation in Weekly Review

**Developing well:** The child comes to the weekly review with observations they generated themselves. "I noticed I kept avoiding my reading this week." "I got upset when Mom corrected me on Tuesday, but I handled it better on Thursday." The observations are specific, honest, and show genuine self-awareness. The child can identify both strengths and struggles without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The weekly review is still entirely mentor-driven. The child answers questions but does not volunteer observations. Answers are generic ("fine," "good," "I don't know") rather than specific. The child cannot identify patterns even when asked.

**How to develop this:** Before the weekly review, give the child five minutes to think on their own: "What do you want to talk about from this week?" Start your own review by sharing a self-observation: "I noticed this week that I got frustrated too quickly when things didn't go as planned. What about you — what did you notice about yourself?" The modeling is critical. A child who has never seen an adult self-reflect will not spontaneously begin self-reflecting.

### 8. Proactive Mistake Ownership

**Developing well:** The child sometimes comes forward about mistakes before they are discovered. Not every time — but it happens, and it is happening more frequently. When they do come forward, it is direct: "I forgot to close the gate and the dog got out. I'm going to go find him." The disclosure is followed by repair, not by extended self-punishment or expectation of praise.

**Developing slowly:** The child never volunteers mistakes. Errors are discovered by others. When confronted, the child may be honest, but the initiative is always external. The concept of coming forward before being caught has not taken root.

**Critical environment factor:** Proactive mistake ownership only develops in an environment where disclosure is safe. If the child's experience is that admitting a mistake produces punishment, anger, or extended disappointment, they will rationally choose concealment. The child who hides mistakes is usually protecting themselves from a response they have learned to fear. Before diagnosing the child, ask: what happens when they come forward? Is the response proportionate and focused on repair? Or is the response emotionally heavy enough that concealment feels safer?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Removing Scaffolding Too Fast
The most common A2 error is removing too many supports simultaneously. The child who has been reminded about chores, guided through task ordering, and coached through every correction cannot have all three supports removed in the same week. Pick one area for increased independence. Let it stabilize. Then expand.

### Removing Scaffolding Too Slowly
The second most common error is the opposite: continuing to manage the child at the A1 level because it is easier and produces fewer short-term failures. A child who is still being reminded about every chore at age nine is being over-managed. The discomfort of watching them forget and then dealing with the natural consequence is the price of their development. If you are unwilling to pay that price, the child remains dependent.

### Mistaking Compliance for Ownership
A child who does their chore when you are in the room and skips it when you are not has compliance, not ownership. This is easy to miss if you are usually present. Test it: leave the house for an hour during chore time. What happens? The answer tells you what you actually have.

### Punishing Proactive Disclosure
If a child comes to you and says "I broke this," and your response is sharp anger or extended consequence, you have just trained them to hide the next thing they break. The response to proactive disclosure should be: acknowledge the honesty briefly, address the mistake proportionately, focus on repair. "Thank you for telling me. Let's clean it up. Be more careful next time." Done.

### Over-Processing Emotions
A2 asks the child to respond to correction without drama. Some mentors inadvertently create the drama by over-processing the child's emotional response: "I can see you're upset. Do you want to talk about how that made you feel? It's okay to be frustrated." This approach, while well-intentioned, teaches the child that correction is an emotional event that requires extended processing. It is not. Correction is information. Receive it. Act on it. Move on. If the child is genuinely distressed — not performing distress but genuinely struggling — a brief acknowledgment ("I know that's hard to hear") followed by the expectation to correct and move on is sufficient.

### Confusing Self-Monitoring with Self-Criticism
When a child begins noticing their own patterns, they may default to harsh self-judgment: "I'm so bad at math" or "I always mess up." This is not self-monitoring — it is self-labeling, which is the opposite of what A2 develops. Self-monitoring is specific and observational: "I notice I avoid math and leave it until last." Self-criticism is global and identity-based: "I'm bad at math." When you hear self-criticism, redirect to observation: "You're not bad at math. What I hear is that you tend to leave it until last. Why do you think that is? What happens when you do it first instead?"

### Comparing Siblings
If you have multiple children in the framework at different levels, resist the comparison. "Your sister does her chores without being reminded — why can't you?" is not motivation. It is humiliation. Each child's trajectory is their own. Compare the child only to their own past performance.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Chore Ownership
**Strong:** Names specific chores with specific standards. Can report honestly on how many days they were independent. "I feed the chickens every morning — fill the feeder and check their water. This week I did it on my own every day except Thursday when I forgot and didn't notice until after lunch."
**Weak:** Vague description. Cannot report on independence honestly, or claims perfection that does not match observed reality.

### Prompt 2: Want vs. Need
**Strong:** Describes a specific moment with honest self-awareness. "I wanted to get the new Lego set at the store, but I knew it wasn't something I needed and we weren't there for that. I was disappointed, but I didn't ask."
**Weak:** Cannot recall an example. Or the example ends with getting the want anyway, suggesting the distinction is not yet producing behavioral change.

### Prompt 3: Correction Response
**Strong:** Describes the correction honestly, including their own imperfect response. "Mom told me I hadn't wiped the table well enough. My first impulse was to say 'Yes I did,' but I stopped and just went and wiped it again. It was hard not to argue."
**Weak:** Cannot recall a correction (unlikely — more likely avoiding the topic). Or describes the correction as unfair without any self-reflection about their response.

### Prompt 4: Productive Waiting
**Strong:** Describes using waiting time purposefully. "We had to wait at the dentist for a long time. I read my book. I didn't ask Mom when we'd be called." Demonstrates initiative.
**Weak:** "I was bored." No evidence of self-directed activity during the wait.

### Prompt 5: Mistake Ownership
**Strong:** Describes a specific instance of coming forward. Shows understanding of why it mattered. "I accidentally knocked over the plant and dirt went everywhere. I told Dad before he saw it and cleaned it up. It was hard because I wanted to just fix it and not say anything."
**Weak:** Cannot recall an instance. Or describes a time they were caught and then told the truth — which is honesty, but not proactive ownership.

### Prompt 6: Self-Directed Task Ordering
**Strong:** Describes the choice and the reasoning. "I did math first because I'm better at it in the morning. Then reading. Then handwriting because it's my least favorite and I can push through it at the end." Shows strategic thinking about their own capacity.
**Weak:** "I just did them in order." No evidence of strategic choice. Or "I did the fun one first" without awareness of the consequences.

### Prompt 7: Self-Observation
**Strong:** Names a specific, honest pattern. "I noticed that when I'm tired, I argue more when I get corrected. It happened twice this week — both times in the afternoon." This level of specificity and honesty at ages 7-9 is exceptional and should be noted, not praised excessively.
**Weak:** "I was good this week." Global and non-specific. No evidence of actual self-observation.
